questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: With reference to the Viksit Bharat– Guarantee for Rozgar and Ajeevika Mission (Gramin): VB-G RAM G Act, 2025, consider the following statements:
I. It guarantees at least 150 days of wage employment per eligible rural household in a financial year.
II. It is implemented as a Central Sector Scheme.
III. To facilitate labour availability during peak agricultural seasons of sowing or harvesting, the act empowers States to pause work for a certain number of days in a year.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Viksit Bharat– Guarantee for Rozgar and Ajeevika Mission (Gramin): VB-G RAM G Act, 2025, consider the following statements: I. It guarantees at least 150 days of wage employment per eligible rural household in a financial year. II. It is implemented as a Central Sector Scheme. III. To facilitate labour availability during peak agricultural seasons of sowing or harvesting, the act empowers States to pause work for a certain number of days in a year. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Explanation: Option b is the correct answer. The **Viksit Bharat–Guarantee for Rozgar and Ajeevika Mission (Gramin) Act, 2025 (VB-G RAM G Act)** is a rural employment legislation enacted by the Parliament of India in December 2025 to establish a revised statutory framework for rural employment and livelihood security in India.
- The Act replaces the earlier **Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act, 2005 (MGNREGA)** and guarantees at least **125 days** of wage employment per financial year to every rural household whose adult members volunteer to undertake unskilled manual work.
- The legislation forms part of the government's broader development vision **Viksit Bharat @2047**, and aims to integrate employment generation with creation of durable rural assets, improved village-level planning, and stronger coordination among rural development programmes.
Statement I is incorrect: Under the VB-G RAM G Act, 2025, the statutory guarantee has been enhanced to at least **125 days (not 150 days)** of wage employment per rural household in a financial year to households whose adult members volunteer to undertake unskilled manual work.
The earlier guarantee of 100 days was provided under the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act, 2005 (MGNREGA). The Act emphasises decentralised and participatory planning at the village level. All works are planned through a bottom-up process.
All works undertaken under the programme must originate from **Viksit Gram Panchayat Plans (VGPPs)**. These plans are:
- Prepared at the **Gram Panchayat level**,
- Developed through participatory planning processes involving the local community, and-Approved by the **Gram Sabha**.
This ensures that the works taken up under the scheme reflect local development priorities and livelihood needs, thereby strengthening the role of grassroots democratic institutions.
Statement II is incorrect: The Act is implemented as a **Centrally Sponsored Scheme** with shared financial responsibility between the Union and the States.
The cost-sharing pattern is structured as follows:
- **60 : 40** (Centre : State) for general category States
- **90 : 10** for Northeastern and Himalayan States
- **100% central funding** for Union Territories without legislatures The Funding Shift: This is a major structural departure from MGNREGA. While MGNREGA was a Central Sector scheme where the Centre bore 100% of unskilled wages, the VB-GRAM G Act is a **Centrally Sponsored Scheme (CSS)**.
Statement III is correct: The Act allows States to **temporarily suspend employment during peak agricultural seasons**, such as sowing or harvesting.
- States may notify a limited number of days (up to around **60 days** in a financial year) when works under the programme may not be provided.
- The objective is to ensure that **rural labour remains available for agricultural activities** during critical farming periods, thereby preventing labour shortages in agriculture.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: Which among the following Articles constitute the 'Golden Triangle' of the Constitution of India, as per the Supreme Court judgment in Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978)?
questions.questionText: Which among the following Articles constitute the 'Golden Triangle' of the Constitution of India, as per the Supreme Court judgment in Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978)?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Explanation: Option a is the correct answer. The concept of the **"Golden Triangle"** of the Constitution was emphasized and explained by the Supreme Court in **Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978)**.
The Court held that **Articles 14, 19, and 21 are interconnected** and together form the core of the fundamental rights protecting individual liberty.
In this case, the Court departed from the earlier view in A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras and held that these rights are not mutually exclusive but interconnected.
It significantly expanded the interpretation of **Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty)** and held that any law depriving a person of life or personal liberty must satisfy the requirements of all three Articles together.
- **Article 14** ensures equality before the law and protection against arbitrary state action.
- **Article 19** guarantees six fundamental freedoms to citizens (such as freedom of speech, movement, association, profession, etc.).
- **Article 21** protects life and personal liberty, stating that no person shall be deprived of these except according to procedure established by law.
The Court ruled that the **"procedure established by law"** under Article 21 must be **just, fair and reasonable**, not arbitrary or oppressive.
Therefore, any law affecting personal liberty must pass a **"triple test"**: 1. It must not be arbitrary (Article 14). 2. It must not impose unreasonable restrictions on freedoms (Article 19).
3. It must follow a fair, just and reasonable procedure (Article 21). Because these three provisions collectively safeguard individual liberty and limit arbitrary state power, they are described as the "Golden Triangle" of the Constitution.
These three articles, like the three sides of a triangle, form the core of the fundamental rights guaranteed to every citizen of India. The concept of the 'Golden Triangle' of the Constitution was later explicitly emphasized in **Minerva Mills v. Union of India (1980)** and reaffirmed in subsequent cases such as Bachan Singh v. State of Punjab (1982) and T.R. Kothandaraman v. Tamil Nadu Water Supply & Drainage Board (1994).

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: With reference to the Departmentally Related Standing Committees (DRSCs) of Parliament, consider the following statements:
I. They consist of members drawn exclusively from the Lok Sabha.
II. A Union Minister is eligible to be nominated as a member of these Committees.
III. These Committees are mandated to examine the Demands for Grants of the concerned ministries and departments before they are discussed and voted upon in the Lok Sabha.
IV. They cannot examine matters of day-today administration of the concerned ministries.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Departmentally Related Standing Committees (DRSCs) of Parliament, consider the following statements: I. They consist of members drawn exclusively from the Lok Sabha. II. A Union Minister is eligible to be nominated as a member of these Committees. III. These Committees are mandated to examine the Demands for Grants of the concerned ministries and departments before they are discussed and voted upon in the Lok Sabha. IV. They cannot examine matters of day-today administration of the concerned ministries. Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Explanation: Option d is the correct answer. The **Departmentally Related Standing Committees (DRSCs)** are a system of parliamentary committees established in 1993 to strengthen the legislative oversight of the executive and improve the scrutiny of government policies and expenditure. These Committees function under the overall framework of the Parliament of India and are attached to specific ministries or departments of the Government of India.
At present, there are **24 DRSCs**, of which **16 function under the Lok Sabha** and **8 under the Rajya Sabha**.

Statement I is incorrect: Each Departmentally Related Standing Committee (DRSC) **does not consist exclusively of members from the Lok Sabha**.
- Every DRSC has **31 members**: o **21 members from the Lok Sabha** o **10 members from the Rajya Sabha**
- Members are nominated by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha and the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha respectively.
- Hence, the committees are **bicameral in composition**, not exclusive to the Lok Sabha. Departmentally Related Standing Committees are mandated to examine the **Demands for Grants** of the concerned ministries and departments.
- After the Union Budget is presented in Parliament, the Demands for Grants of different ministries are referred to the respective DRSCs for detailed scrutiny.
- The committees analyze these demands and submit their reports to Parliament before the Demands for Grants are discussed and voted upon in the Lok Sabha.
- However, the committees **cannot vote on or amend these demands**; they only provide recommendations.
- Moreover, the recommendations of these committees are **advisory in nature** and hence not binding on the Parliament.
Statement II is incorrect: A **Union Minister is not eligible** to be nominated as a member of any DRSC; further, if a member is appointed as a Minister after nomination, he/she ceases to be a member of the Committee, ensuring separation between executive and legislative oversight.
Statement III is correct: Department-related Standing Committees are **mandated to examine the Demands for Grants** of the concerned ministries and departments.
- After the Union Budget is presented in Parliament, the Demands for Grants of different ministries are referred to the respective DRSCs for detailed scrutiny.
- The committees analyze these demands and submit their reports to Parliament before the Demands for Grants are discussed and voted upon in the Lok Sabha.
- However, the committees **cannot vote on or amend** these demands; they only provide recommendations.
- Moreover, the recommendations of these committees are **advisory in nature** and hence not binding on the Parliament.
Statement IV is correct: Departmentally Related Standing Committees **cannot examine matters relating to the day-today administration** of the ministries or departments concerned.
- This limitation ensures that the committees focus on **broad policy issues, budgetary scrutiny, and legislative oversight**, rather than routine executive decisions.
- Additionally, these committees should not generally consider matters that are already being examined by other parliamentary committees, so as to avoid duplication of work and maintain institutional efficiency.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: 'Right to Education' is incorporated under which of the following categories of Fundamental Rights?
questions.questionText: 'Right to Education' is incorporated under which of the following categories of Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Explanation: Option c is the correct answer. The Indian Constitution organizes Fundamental Rights into six broad categories. The Right to Education is guaranteed under **Article 21A** of the Constitution of India, which forms part of the group of Fundamental Rights placed under the **Right to Freedom**, which spans from **Article 19 to Article 22**.
- This provision makes **free and compulsory education a Fundamental Right** for all children in the age group of **6 to 14 years**.
Article 21A states: "The State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children of the age of six to fourteen years in such manner as the State may, by law, determine"
- The Supreme Court, in the landmark case of **Unnikrishnan vs State of Kerala (1993)** emphasized the importance of education as a Fundamental Right.
This judgment laid the foundation for the incorporation of Article 21A.
- The provision was inserted into the Constitution by the **86th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2002**.
Through this amendment:
- **Article 21A** was added to make elementary education a Fundamental Right.
- **Article 45** of the Constitution of India was modified to direct the State to provide early childhood care and education for children below six years of age.
- **Article 51A (k)** of the Constitution of India was inserted as a Fundamental Duty, making it the duty of parents or guardians to provide opportunities for education to children between 6 and 14 years.
To implement the constitutional mandate under Article 21A, Parliament enacted the **Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009**, which came into force in 2010.
Knowledge Base: Category of Fundamental Rights-Articles-Key Provisions Right to Equality-Articles 14–18-Equality before law and equal protection of laws (Art. 14); Prohibition of discrimination (Art. 15); Equality of opportunity in public employment (Art. 16); Abolition of Untouchability (Art. 17); Abolition of titles (Art. 18) Right to Freedom-Articles 19–22, 21A-Six freedoms (Art. 19): speech, assembly, association, movement, residence, profession; Protection in respect of conviction (Art. 20); Right to life and personal liberty (Art. 21); Right to education (Art. 21A); Protection against arrest and detention (Art. 22) Right against Exploitation-Articles 23–24-Prohibition of human trafficking and forced labour (Art. 23); Prohibition of child labour in hazardous employment (Art. 24) Right to Freedom of Religion-Articles 25–28-Freedom of conscience and religion (Art. 25); Freedom to manage religious affairs (Art. 26); No taxation for promotion of religion (Art. 27); No religious instruction in certain institutions (Art. 28) Cultural and Educational Rights-Articles 29–30-Protection of language, script and culture of minorities (Art. 29); Right of minorities to establish and administer educational institutions (Art. 30) Right to Constitutional Remedies-Article 32-Right to move courts for enforcement of Fundamental Rights (writ jurisdiction of Supreme Court)

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: Which of the following articles of the Constitution of India provides for securing the uniform civil code throughout the territory of India?
questions.questionText: Which of the following articles of the Constitution of India provides for securing the uniform civil code throughout the territory of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Explanation: Option b is the correct answer.

Option b is correct: **Article 44** of the Constitution of India is a part of the **Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP)** contained in Part IV of the Constitution.
It directs the State to endeavour to secure for the citizens a **Uniform Civil Code (UCC)** throughout the territory of India.
- UCC aims to ensure that all citizens, regardless of their religion, are treated equally under a **national civil code**.
- This code encompasses various aspects of personal law, including marriage, divorce, maintenance, inheritance, adoption, and property succession.
It is founded on the principle that **law and religion should be separate** in modern society.
- Being a Directive Principle, it is **non-justiciable**, i.e., not enforceable by courts, but it is fundamental in the governance of the country and serves as a guiding principle for the State.
Option a is incorrect: **Article 45** of the Constitution of India: It directs the State to provide **early childhood care and education** for all children until they complete the age of six years.
This provision was modified by the **86th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2002**, after which free and compulsory education for children aged 6–14 years was shifted to Article 21A of the Constitution of India.
Option c is incorrect: **Article 46** of the Constitution of India: It directs the State to promote the **educational and economic interests of Scheduled Castes (SCs), Scheduled Tribes (STs)**, and other weaker sections, and to protect them from social injustice and all forms of exploitation.
Option d is incorrect: **Article 42** of the Constitution of India: It directs the State to make provisions for **just and humane conditions of work** and for **maternity relief**, reflecting the Constitution's commitment to labour welfare and social justice.
Knowledge Base: Uniform Civil Code in India:
- After its liberation in 1961, **Goa retained the Portuguese Civil Code**, which continues to function as a Uniform Civil Code (UCC) applicable to all religions in the state.
The Supreme Court in 2019 hailed Goa as a "shining example" of an Indian State which has a functioning UCC.
- In 2024, **Uttarakhand became the first state** in India after independence to enact a Uniform Civil Code, marking a significant development in the implementation of the constitutional directive under Article 44 of the Constitution of India.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: With reference to the Parliament of India, the term "guillotine" refers to a process by which:
questions.questionText: With reference to the Parliament of India, the term "guillotine" refers to a process by which:

options.a: A Money Bill is deemed to have been passed if the Rajya Sabha does not return it to Lok Sabha within fourteen days.
options.b: The Finance Bill is taken up for voting immediately after the presentation of the Union Budget.
options.c: The Speaker orders the immediate suspension of a Member of Lok Sabha for engaging in unparliamentary conduct.
options.d: All outstanding Demands for Grants are put to vote at once, without discussion, on the last day allotted for their consideration.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
Explanation: Option d is the correct answer.

Option d is correct: In the financial procedure of the Indian Parliament, after the presentation of the Union Budget, the expenditure proposals of each Ministry are presented in the form of **Demands for Grants**.
These Demands for Grants are **discussed and voted upon only in the Lok Sabha**, because the power of the purse rests with the directly elected House of the people.
The **Rajya Sabha does not vote** on Demands for Grants. A specific number of days during the Budget Session are allotted for the discussion of these Demands for Grants of different Ministries.
Members of Parliament may examine them in detail and may also move **cut motions** to reduce the amount of a Demand for Grant as a means of criticizing government policy or expenditure.
However, the number of Ministries and Demands for Grants is very large, while the time available for discussion in the House is limited. As a result, it is often not possible to discuss every Demand for Grant individually. To ensure that the budgetary process is completed within the scheduled time, the parliamentary device known as **"Guillotine"** is used.
- **On the last day allotted for the discussion of Demands for Grants, the Speaker of the Lok Sabha puts all the remaining Demands for Grants, whether discussed or not, to vote together at once without further debate.** Once these Demands are voted, they are considered to have been approved by the House.
This mechanism ensures the timely completion of the financial business of Parliament, allowing the government to subsequently introduce and pass the **Appropriation Bill**, which authorizes the withdrawal of funds from the Consolidated Fund of India to meet the approved expenditures of the government.
Option a is incorrect: This refers to the **special legislative procedure for a Money Bill**. According to Article 109 of the Constitution, when a Money Bill is passed by the Lok Sabha and transmitted to the Rajya Sabha, the Upper House must return it within **14 days** with its recommendations.
If it fails to do so, the bill is deemed to have been passed by both Houses in the form originally passed by the Lok Sabha. This constitutional provision relates to the legislative process for Money Bills and is not connected with the guillotine procedure.
Option b is incorrect: The **Finance Bill** contains the government's taxation proposals and is introduced in the Lok Sabha after the presentation of the Union Budget.
However, it is **not immediately put to vote**. Like other bills, it goes through the legislative stages of discussion, consideration, and passing.
Therefore, the immediate voting on the Finance Bill after the presentation of the Budget does not describe the guillotine procedure.

Option c is incorrect: The **Speaker of the Lok Sabha possesses disciplinary powers** to maintain order and decorum in the House, including suspending a Member for grossly disorderly or unparliamentary conduct.
Such suspension may occur under the rules of procedure governing parliamentary discipline. However, this power is related to maintaining order in the House and has **no connection with the budgetary voting procedure known as guillotine**.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: Which of the following best describes the term 'Republic' as enshrined in the Preamble of the Indian Constitution?
questions.questionText: Which of the following best describes the term 'Republic' as enshrined in the Preamble of the Indian Constitution?

options.a: The government is formed by representatives elected by the people.
options.b: The Union Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the House of People.
options.c: The head of the State in India is elected and does not hold office by hereditary succession.
options.d: Political authority is exercised in accordance with the provisions of the Constitution of India.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
Explanation: Option c is the correct answer. The term **"Republic"** in the Preamble denotes a form of government in which the highest constitutional authority of the State is **not hereditary but elective**.
In other words, the **head of the State is chosen by the people**, either directly or indirectly, for a fixed tenure. In the Indian context, this principle is reflected in the office of the **President of India**, who is **indirectly elected** by an electoral college consisting of elected representatives of Parliament and State Legislatures.
This ensures that the ultimate source of authority remains the people, even though the election is not direct. The idea of a Republic also carries a deeper normative significance:
- It affirms the **complete absence of monarchy and hereditary privilege** in public office.
- It establishes the principle of **political equality**, whereby every citizen is eligible to hold the highest office of the State, subject to constitutional qualifications.
- It reinforces the concept of **popular sovereignty**, as all public offices derive legitimacy from the will of the people.
Thus, "Republic" in the Indian Preamble emphasizes that no individual can claim the highest office by birth, and that public authority is based on election, constitutional provisions, and the consent of the governed.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: The term 'Secular' is mentioned in which of the following part/s of the Constitution of India?
I. Preamble
II. Directive Principles of State Policy
III. Fundamental Rights
IV. Fundamental Duties
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: The term 'Secular' is mentioned in which of the following part/s of the Constitution of India? I. Preamble II. Directive Principles of State Policy III. Fundamental Rights IV. Fundamental Duties Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Explanation: Option b is the correct answer. **Secularism** is one of the fundamental principles of the constitutional framework of India. In the Indian context, secularism implies that the State has **no official religion** and maintains **equal respect for all religions** while guaranteeing freedom of conscience and religious practice to all individuals.
Unlike the strict separation model followed in some Western countries, Indian secularism allows the State to intervene in the secular aspects of religious institutions to ensure social reform and equality.
The judiciary has also recognized secularism as a part of the **basic structure of the Constitution**, notably in the landmark judgment of **S. R. Bommai v. Union of India**.
Option I is correct: The term "Secular" is **explicitly mentioned in the Preamble** of the Constitution of India. It was inserted by the **42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976**.
Before this amendment, the Preamble described India as a "Sovereign Democratic Republic." After the amendment, it reads: **"Sovereign Socialist Secular Democratic Republic."** The inclusion of the word "Secular" formally affirmed that the Indian State does not uphold any official religion and treats all religions equally, maintaining neutrality among them.
Option II is incorrect: The **Directive Principles of State Policy (Part IV, Articles 36–51) do not explicitly contain the word "secular."** However, some directives indirectly reflect secular ideals. For example:
- **Article 44** directs the State to endeavour to secure a **Uniform Civil Code (UCC)** for all citizens, which aims at creating a common set of civil laws irrespective of religion.
- Several DPSPs emphasize social justice, equality and reform, which align with secular governance. Despite these indirect connections, the term "secular" itself does not appear anywhere in Part IV.
Option III is correct: The term "secular" **appears explicitly in Article 25(2)a)** under Part III (Fundamental Rights). Article 25 guarantees freedom of conscience and the right to freely profess, practice and propagate religion.
Article 25(2)a) provides that the State may regulate or restrict: **"any economic, financial, political or other secular activity which may be associated with religious practice."** Here, the Constitution clearly distinguishes religious practices from **secular activities** connected with religion, allowing the State to regulate the latter.
This reflects the Indian model of secularism, where the State may intervene in the secular aspects of religious institutions while protecting freedom of religion.
Other provisions reinforcing secularism in Fundamental Rights include:
- **Article 26**
- Freedom to manage religious affairs
- **Article 27**
- Freedom from taxation for promotion of any particular religion
- **Article 28**
- Freedom from religious instruction in certain educational institutions
Option IV is incorrect: The **Fundamental Duties (Part IVA, Article 51A)** were introduced by the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act. Although they promote values consistent with secularism, the **term "secular" itself is not mentioned** in this part. For instance:
- **Article 51A(e)** directs citizens to promote harmony and the spirit of common brotherhood amongst all the people of India transcending religious differences.
While this duty supports the secular ethos of the Constitution, the word "secular" does not explicitly occur in the Fundamental Duties.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: Consider the following types of cases:
I. Cases related to the Compoundable criminal offences
II. Cases related to the Non-compoundable criminal offences
III. Land acquisition cases.
IV. Cases pertaining to recovery of money by the banks and other financial institutions.
How many of the above come under the subject matter jurisdiction of the Lok Adalats in India?
questions.questionText: Consider the following types of cases: I. Cases related to the Compoundable criminal offences II. Cases related to the Non-compoundable criminal offences III. Land acquisition cases. IV. Cases pertaining to recovery of money by the banks and other financial institutions. How many of the above come under the subject matter jurisdiction of the Lok Adalats in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Explanation: Option c is the correct answer. **Lok Adalats** are statutory Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) forums established under the **Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987** to provide speedy, inexpensive, and amicable settlement of disputes through conciliation and compromise.
They are organized by the National Legal Services Authority, State Legal Services Authorities, District Legal Services Authorities, and Taluk Legal Services Committees.
A key feature of Lok Adalats is that their **awards are deemed to be decrees of a civil court** and are **final and binding** on the parties, with **no appeal lying against the award** before any court of law.
However, they are free to initiate litigation by approaching the court of appropriate jurisdiction by filing a case. **Nature of Cases to be Referred to Lok Adalat** 1. Any case pending before any court.
2. Any dispute which has not been brought before any court and is likely to be filed before the court. Provided that any matter relating to an offence not compoundable under the law shall not be settled in Lok Adalat.
Option I is correct: **Compoundable criminal offences** Lok Adalats are empowered to take up criminal cases that are compoundable under law, i.e., **offences where compromise between parties is legally permissible** (e.g., simple hurt, defamation).
Such cases can be settled amicably without a full trial. Option II is incorrect: **Non-compoundable criminal offences** The Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987 **clearly prohibits** Lok Adalats from dealing with non-compoundable offences.
These include serious crimes (e.g., murder, rape), where compromise is not legally allowed and adjudication by a regular court is necessary. Option III is correct: **Land acquisition cases** Disputes relating to compensation in land acquisition matters are **civil in nature** and are frequently referred to Lok Adalats for settlement.
Option IV is correct: **Cases pertaining to banks and other financial institutions** Lok Adalats regularly settle **bank recovery cases, loan disputes, and financial claims**, particularly in pre-litigation Lok Adalats, to reduce court pendency.
Knowledge Base: WHAT ARE THE CATEGORIES OF CASES SETTLED IN LOK ADALAT? 1. Motor Vehicle Accident Compensation cases. 2. Land acquisition cases.
3. Cases pertaining to banks and other financial institutions. 4. Cases by and against Government and other Local bodies such as Panchayat, Municipal Corporation, Electricity Board etc.
5. Matrimonial and maintenance cases. 6. Labour Disputes, Industrial Disputes and the cases under Workmen Compensation Act. 7. Cases under Consumer Protection Act. 8. Criminal cases wherein the offences are lawfully compoundable. 9. Disaster Compensation.
10. Forest Act cases.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: With reference to recent international agreements/initiatives, consider the following pairs:
I. High Ambition Coalition for Nature and People (2021) - Advocating for limiting global warming to 1.5°C above the pre-industrial level and strengthening mitigation commitments
II. Jaipur Declaration (2025) - Cooperation on crossborder data flows and digital sovereignty among Global South nations
III. Pax Silica Declaration (2025) - Securing semiconductor supply chains and critical technology cooperation
IV. Seoul Statement (2025) - Prevention of zoonotic diseases emerging from rainforest ecosystems
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.questionText: With reference to recent international agreements/initiatives, consider the following pairs: I. High Ambition Coalition for Nature and People (2021) - Advocating for limiting global warming to 1.5°C above the pre-industrial level and strengthening mitigation commitments II. Jaipur Declaration (2025) - Cooperation on crossborder data flows and digital sovereignty among Global South nations III. Pax Silica Declaration (2025) - Securing semiconductor supply chains and critical technology cooperation IV. Seoul Statement (2025) - Prevention of zoonotic diseases emerging from rainforest ecosystems How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Explanation: Option a is the correct answer. Pair I is incorrect: The **High Ambition Coalition for Nature and People (HAC for N&P)**, winner of the 2024 Earthshot Prize's "Revive our Ocean" category, is the largest multilateral coalition united to protect Nature.
It was officially launched in 2021. With 124 member countries, it is leading the historic effort to **conserve and protect at least 30% of the world's land and 30% of the ocean by the year 2030 ("30×30")** a key target of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework.
Established in 2023, the permanent HAC for N&P Secretariat has made significant strides in supporting its members to implement this global target, aiming to halt and reverse biodiversity loss and put nature on a path to recovery.
**High Ambition Coalition (2014)** The High Ambition Coalition (HAC) is an alliance of countries that **advocates strong and ambitious climate action** within international climate negotiations under the UN climate regime.
- It emerged during the negotiations leading to the Paris Agreement at the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change process.
- The coalition was initially spearheaded by the Marshall Islands and later included countries from different regions such as the EU, small island states, and several developing countries.
- The key objective of the coalition is to raise the ambition of global climate action, particularly by: o Advocating the **1.5°C temperature limit** above pre-industrial levels.
o Strengthening mitigation commitments of countries. o Ensuring periodic enhancement of Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs). o Supporting long-term goals such as net-zero greenhouse-gas emissions. Pair II is incorrect: **Jaipur Declaration (2025)** The Jaipur Declaration (2025) is related to **resource efficiency and circular economy**, not digital governance.
- It was adopted at the 12th Regional 3R and Circular Economy Forum in Asia and the Pacific.
- The declaration focuses on promoting sustainable material consumption and waste management through the **3R approach: Reduce, Reuse, Recycle**.
- Its broader objective is to accelerate the transition towards a circular economy in Asia-Pacific countries, improving:
o resource efficiency o sustainable production and consumption o waste reduction o climate-friendly economic systems. Therefore, the subject given in the question (cross-border data flows and digital sovereignty) is incorrect, making the pair wrongly matched.
Pair III is correct: **Pax Silica Declaration (2025):** Pax Silica is a **US-led international initiative** launched in December 2025.
- It aims to build **secure and resilient supply chains for semiconductors, artificial intelligence infrastructure, and critical minerals**.
- The initiative seeks to reduce overconcentration in global supply chains, prevent economic coercion, and ensure that emerging technologies are developed and governed by open, democratic societies.
- The broader objective is to strengthen technological cooperation and economic security among partner countries in the emerging global technology competition.
- Recently, India officially joined the initiative by signing the declaration in February, 2026, at the India AI Impact Summit Pair IV is incorrect: **Seoul Statement (2025)** The Seoul Statement (2025) is actually related to **global governance and standard-setting for Artificial Intelligence (AI)**.
It is **not related to prevention of zoonotic diseases** emerging from rainforest ecosystems-It was issued in December 2025 at the International AI Standards Summit in Seoul, South Korea.
- The statement was jointly released by three major international standards bodies: o International Organization for Standardization (ISO) o International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) o International Telecommunication Union (ITU).
- The summit brought together over 300 participants from about 65 countries including governments, industry, academia, civil society and international organizations.
**Purpose of the Seoul Statement**
- The declaration establishes a shared global vision for the development of **trustworthy and human-centred AI standards**.
- It emphasizes that international standards should help ensure AI is: inclusive and open, safe and secure, sustainable and fair and human-centric, and that AI technologies should benefit society while respecting human rights and global development goals.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: Which one of the following is among the essential features of a federal government?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is among the essential features of a federal government?

options.a: The separation of powers between the legislature, executive, and judiciary.
options.b: The Constitutional/statutory division of powers between the Union and regional governments.
options.c: A form of government where the executive is responsible to the legislature.
options.d: The existence of a multi-party system with periodic, free, and fair elections.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
Explanation: Option b is the correct answer.

Option b is correct: A **federal system** is fundamentally characterised by a **dual polity**, wherein powers are distributed between the Union (central) government and the regional (state/provincial) governments through a Constitution.
This **division of powers is constitutionally entrenched**, meaning that the authority of each level of government is derived directly from the Constitution itself and cannot be unilaterally altered by either level.
- In the Indian context, this distribution is clearly laid down in the **Seventh Schedule (Article 246)**, which demarcates subjects into the Union List, State List, and Concurrent List.
- Each level of government exercises **autonomous jurisdiction** within its assigned sphere, ensuring a balance between unity and regional diversity.
- Moreover, this constitutional division is upheld by the supremacy of the Constitution and protected through mechanisms such as judicial review, which ensures that neither level encroaches upon the domain of the other.
Thus, the **division of powers** is the indispensable and defining feature that gives a federal system its structural identity.

Option a is incorrect: The **separation of powers** refers to the functional division of authority among the three organs of government: the legislature (law-making), the executive (law implementation), and the judiciary (law interpretation).
This principle aims to prevent the concentration of power in a single authority and to ensure a system of checks and balances. However, this arrangement concerns the **horizontal distribution of powers** among organs of government, not the vertical distribution of powers between different levels of government.
It can exist in both unitary and federal systems, and therefore is **not a defining feature of a federal structure**.

Option c is incorrect: This feature describes the **parliamentary form of government**, in which the Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the legislature and must retain the confidence of the lower house to remain in office.
It concerns the relationship between the executive and the legislature within a political system. While parliamentary government may exist in both federal states (such as India and Australia) and unitary states (such as the United Kingdom), it is **not an essential feature of federalism**.
Option d is incorrect: A multi-party system along with periodic, free, and fair elections is a hallmark of a **democratic political system**, ensuring political competition, representation, and accountability of the government to the electorate.
However, these features relate to the nature of the political system (democracy) rather than the structural organization of governmental authority.
Both federal and unitary states may operate under multi-party democratic systems, and therefore this feature **does not define or determine a federal structure of government**.

statementLines: With reference to the Supreme Court of India,
which one of the following statements is not correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Supreme Court of India, which one of the following statements is not correct?

options.a: The Chief Justice of India, with the previous consent of the President, may request an eligible judge of a High Court to sit and act as an ad hoc judge of the Supreme Court.
options.b: Parliament can increase the number of judges in the Supreme Court of India through legislation.
options.c: The Chief Justice of India, with the approval of the President, may appoint a place in addition to Delhi for the Court to sit.
options.d: The President may appoint a retired judge of the Supreme Court to sit and act as a judge of the Supreme Court, with the consent of such a judg

questions.explanationMarkdown:
Explanation: Option d is the correct answer.

Option a is correct: Under **Article 127** of the Constitution, if there is a lack of quorum of permanent judges in the Supreme Court, the **Chief Justice of India (CJI)** may, with the **previous consent of the President** and after consultation with the Chief Justice of the High Court concerned, request a judge of a High Court who is duly qualified to be appointed as a judge of the Supreme Court to sit and act as an **ad hoc judge** of the Court.
While serving in this capacity, such a judge possesses all the jurisdiction, powers, and privileges of a judge of the Supreme Court.

Option b is correct: Although Article 124 establishes the Supreme Court, the **number of judges is not permanently fixed** in the Constitution.
Instead, **Parliament is empowered to determine the strength of the Court by law**. Accordingly, Parliament has enacted the **Supreme Court (Number of Judges) Act, 1956**, which allows the number of judges to be increased through amendments to this Act as the workload of the Court grows.
At present, the Supreme Court consists of **thirty-four judges** (one chief justice and thirty-three other judges).

Option c is correct: According to **Article 130** of the Constitution, the seat of the Supreme Court is in **Delhi**. However, the **Chief Justice of India**, with the **approval of the President**, may appoint another place or places where the Court may hold its sittings.
This provision allows flexibility in case the Court needs to sit elsewhere.

Option d is incorrect: Under **Article 128** of the Constitution, it is **not the President** who appoints such judges. Instead, the **Chief Justice of India** may request a retired judge of the Supreme Court or a retired judge of a High Court who is qualified to be a Supreme Court judge to sit and act as a judge of the Supreme Court.
This can be done only with the **prior consent of the President** and the concerned judge. Thus, the initiative lies with the **Chief Justice of India, not the President**.
